title: Simulation: Government 3.0
subtitle: A narrated, animated simulation of the Government 3.0 architecture.
abstract: The Compression Problem argued that written briefings over-compress: the ideas that matter most are exactly the ones a page of text cannot carry, while human visual and spatial reasoning sits unused. Building on this, this set of simulations present the Government 3.0 architecture, previously introduced in the Four Approaches. Watch agents gain delegated, observable access to securely interact with government tools and systems. Agents create dynamic interactions, built just in time, for the right audience. Feedback and monitoring allows the system to detect friction and to resolve this with recommendations to policy drivers to reduce red tape.

hero_image.alt: A simple geometric simulation mid-play: small orange agents moving between people, gateways, and systems on a cream stage, with a camera frame around the action.
hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial cover illustration of a minimal geometric stage seen slightly from above: small orange circular agents moving along curved paths between simple navy rectangles and human figures, one ephemeral interface card materializing mid-air, a subtle camera frame around the scene suggesting a simulation in progress. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text, no logos.

slides.title: Simulation: Government 3.0
slides.caption: Paper 19 · Technical Appendix
slides.subcaption: Some transformations are better witnessed than described.
slides.text: Simulation: Government 3.0. The Compression Problem argued that written briefings over-compress, and that our visual and spatial reasoning is the channel we leave unused. This paper acts on the argument. It contains a narrated, animated simulation of the Government 3.0 architecture. Press play, and watch the transformation instead of reading about it.

slides.title: Why simulate

visual_config.text: You leave the Ferrari in the garage and ignore our millennia of evolution in visual, spatial, and auditory understanding.

slides.text: Why simulate. A decision-maker asked to approve a transformation of this scale deserves better than a two-page note standing in for thousands of pages of method. A simulation compresses differently: it keeps the structure, the motion, and the cause and effect, and lets the eye do the verifying. Timed narration carries the argument while the picture carries the system.

slides.title: The four stages
slides.caption: What the simulation shows

items.label: Augment
items.desc: Agents add governed APIs and role-aware locks to hundreds of closed systems.

items.label: Trust
items.desc: Delegated identity for every agent, and an audit ledger for every action.

items.label: Direct
items.desc: Any public servant instantiates an agent and instructs it in plain language.

items.label: Dissolve
items.desc: Interfaces are built for the task and disappear when the task is done.

slides.text: The simulation tells the story in stages. First, augmentation: agents add governed doorways to the hundreds of systems that have none. Second, trust: every agent carries a delegated identity, and every action lands in an audit ledger. Third, direction: a public servant creates an agent and instructs it in plain language. Fourth, the interface itself becomes ephemeral, built for the task and gone when the task is done.

slides.title: The destination
slides.caption: Government 3.0

left.label: Today
left.body: Thousands of fixed applications, each built, patched, and secured on its own.

right.label: Government 3.0
right.body: People and agents on top, a governed platform underneath, and repeatable rules at the core, with legislation as the ground truth.

slides.text: The destination is the architecture the simulation ends on. People and their agents on top. Underneath, the platform Technology and Innovation operates: APIs, gateways, identity, observability, tasking, ephemeral interfaces, knowledge, and tools. At the core, government in its purest form: rules, policies, and permissions, applied as repeatable rules so every decision is consistent, with legislation as the ground truth. And because the system watches its own friction, red tape becomes a maintenance task with people in command.

slides.title: A reusable engine
slides.caption: Built once, used again

items.label: Data-driven
items.desc: One JSON file holds the shapes, the timing, the camera, and the script.

items.label: Audio-locked
items.desc: The narration clock drives the animation, so voice and motion never drift.

items.label: Bilingual
items.desc: Every label, caption, and narration line carries English and French.

slides.text: The simulation player is a reusable engine. One JSON file declares the shapes, the movements, the camera, the captions, and the narration for each chapter, and the engine renders it with the narration as the master clock. The next concept we need to explain gets a new data file, and no new software. This is the briefing format The Compression Problem asked for.

blocks.text: Simulations provide meaningful alternatives to text briefings. These following simulations present the core concepts introduced in [The Four Approaches](/paper/zgym1), which described Government 3.0 as a future model of civic interaction with government IT systems. It contains a narrated, animated simulation of the approaches and steps to achieve the objective of a responsive government digital architecture.

Decision-makers are frequently asked to approve multi-year transformations. They review project plans, costs, and risks in a briefing note, written, format with a slide presentation. The information is significantly compressed, thousands of pages of design, architecture, business rules, and regulations, compressed into a high-level plan or ten-slide PowerPoint presentation. Decision-makers must rapidly make decisions based on the note and have little opportunity to see the bigger vision or to interact with the process. 

A visual and interactive simulation presents this information in a format which is intended to be clear, well sequenced, and builds on visual, audio, and textual understanding of the problem and the solution. It keeps the system whole and speeds it up, so five years of staged work plays out in eight narrated minutes. The eye verifies what the text could only assert.

Your spatial, visual, auditory, temporal, and even sensory intelligence is ignored when reading a text briefing. The simulation extends human engagement and understanding into different media formats. The simulation content is highly accessible for any type of learner or leader.

The format matters as much as the content. The narration is timed to the animation, so the voice explains each motion as it happens, in either language. The captions reinforce the one idea on screen. The camera moves sparingly, and only to direct attention. Nothing in the picture is decorative. Every shape is a real part of the architecture, and every movement is a real step in the plan.

blocks.title: Government 3.0, simulated
blocks.caption: Press Play for the full narrated walk-through, or use the chapter chips to jump. The transcript button shows the narration as text. Expand for full screen. The simulation is bilingual and follows the site language.

blocks.text: The same story is told twice on this page. The player above draws the architecture as a clean animated diagram. The one below tells it as an isometric game board: buildings stand in for systems, sprites stand in for people and their agents, and the camera glides over a small world while the same narration plays. Both run the same eight chapters on the same audio clock. Choose whichever way of watching suits you. Future simulations will ship in both styles.

blocks.title: Government 3.0, the game board
blocks.caption: The same eight chapters on an isometric game board, with gpt-image-2 sprites for the people, agents, and buildings. Press Play, or use the chapter chips to jump; the narration, captions, and transcript match the diagram player above.

blocks.text: The third player below leaves the rails entirely. The same eight chapters and the same narration play out in a navigable three-dimensional environment: buildings rise and sink, the gateway colonnade climbs out of the ground, and the camera is yours to take. Drag to orbit, scroll to zoom, and click any glowing marker to pause the ride and learn what that element is. Each marker opens a panel with the glossary definition, an excerpt from the paper that introduced the idea, and a link to read further. When AI can join these briefings live, that panel becomes a conversation; today it carries the pre-generated knowledge, so the future is visible in the present tense.

blocks.title: Government 3.0, in three dimensions
blocks.caption: The 3D engine: ride the chapters or explore freely. Click the ⓘ markers to learn about gateways, agents, the audit ledger, governed APIs, and the rules core, with definitions and excerpts drawn from across the collection.

blocks.text: The fourth player abandons modern visual language altogether. The same eight chapters and the same narration play across a continuous embroidered tapestry in the Bayeux tradition: clerks carry scrolls between crumbling towers, woven automata receive their heraldic badges, and the legislature radiates golden thread. Each panel weaves itself into the cloth as its chapter begins. Two things here exist nowhere else. Hold the lens over the embroidery and it reveals the engineer's underdrawing beneath the art, the same composition redrawn as construction lines on vellum. And the gold medallions in the margins open into parchment annotations, each carrying a line from the collection and a link to the paper it came from.

blocks.title: Government 3.0, the woven chronicle
blocks.caption: The tapestry engine: eight embroidered panels woven in as the narration plays. Hold the lens over the cloth to see the engineering beneath the embroidery; open the gold medallions for the marginalia.

blocks.text: The fifth player tells the story in light. Ten thousand particles condense into the estate itself: nine ministry towers with lit windows, the corporate tools under their cloud, public servants walking information across the gap by hand. Every event in the narration has a visible counterpart. When the first agent flies at a ministry system, a red lattice flashes where it strikes and the comet deflects away: the missing machine interface, made visible. Build agents then stamp golden doorways onto each tower and the wall dissolves into dust. Every action writes a glowing line into the audit ledger. A public servant speaks an agent into being, the words themselves condensing into the worker, and an interface assembles in mid-air for one decision before scattering back to nothing. In the finale the whole estate reorganizes into the destination architecture, people above, platform below, rules at the radiant core, finds its own red knots of friction, and heals them in a single golden wave. Drag to orbit, scroll to zoom; the field stirs gently under your pointer. And the whole telling exists twice: a button flips the night void into its inversion, the same ten thousand particles redrawn as ink stippling on the collection's cream.

blocks.title: Government 3.0, drawn in light
blocks.caption: The light-field engine: the same eight chapters, with the estate, the agents, and every action condensed out of particles. Watch the wall flash red where agents strike it, the doors stamp gold, the ledger write itself line by line, and the final wave heal the friction. Drag to orbit, scroll to zoom; the Light button redraws the field as ink on paper.

blocks.text: The sixth player turns the story into architecture. It is the most demanding of the six on the machine, and the most cinematic: a cathedral of glass and light, lit by a hand-built bloom and reflected on a black mirror floor. The estate begins as dark, opaque monoliths — systems sealed so completely that no door a machine could open exists. Augmentation does not bolt a doorway onto the stone; it transmutes the stone itself, and each system crystallizes from rough rock into a faceted gem that light passes through. Opacity becoming observability, made literal. Agents are motes of green light that carry golden rings of delegated identity; the audit ledger inscribes itself, line by line, into a tall slab of glass; and at the centre the rules core ignites, throwing light up through the whole structure before a single golden wave finds the red veins of friction and heals them. You can fly the cathedral freely, and click any crystal to pause and read what it represents, with the glossary definition and the papers behind it.

blocks.title: Government 3.0, a cathedral of light
blocks.caption: The cathedral engine: the same eight chapters rendered as glass and light, with bloom, reflection, and a custom crystal shader. Watch the sealed monoliths crystallize from opaque stone into faceted glass, the ledger inscribe itself, the rules core ignite, and a golden wave heal the friction. Drag to orbit, scroll to zoom, click a crystal to learn what it is.

blocks.text: The simulation opens on the estate as it exists today: corporate tools in one world, ministry systems in dozens of others, and people carrying information across the gaps by hand. Agents arrive easily enough, as a simple app, a browser extension, or an assistant inside the tools you already use. Then they hit the wall that defines the whole problem: most government systems offer no interface a machine can use.

The middle chapters show the four stages that remove the wall. Build agents add governed APIs, each with a role-aware lock, to hundreds of closed systems. Identity and observability arrive next, so every agent carries a delegated badge and every action lands in an audit ledger. Then one public servant instantiates an agent, instructs it in plain language, and refines the plan through conversation. Finally the agent works across the gateways and builds an interface only when it needs its person: a small screen for one decision, gone when the decision is made.

blocks.title: The four stages the simulation walks through
rows: Build agents add governed APIs and role-aware access control to hundreds of legacy systems.
The estate becomes reachable by machine, safely, for the first time.
Every agent gets a delegated identity tied to a person; every action is recorded in an audit ledger.
Agents can be trusted to act, because every act is attributable and replayable.
A public servant creates an agent, describes the task in plain language, and iterates on the plan.
Directing agents becomes an everyday skill rather than a technical specialty.
The agent works across gateways and generates a just-in-time interface for each human decision.
The fixed application layer, and its maintenance burden, stops being needed.
blocks.caption: Each stage builds on the one before it. The order is the dependency chain: no delegation without doors, no tasking without trust.

blocks.text: The final chapters pull the camera back. People and their agents sit on top. Technology and Innovation operates the platform underneath: the APIs, the gateways, the identity and delegation, the observability, the tasking, the ephemeral interfaces, the knowledge and memory, and the tools. At the centre sits government in its purest form: rules, policies, and permissions. Legislation stays the ground truth, and the platform applies it as repeatable rules, so a decision comes out the same way for every person it touches. The closing chapter shows the property that makes the architecture durable. The system watches its own friction, drafts the fix, and brings it to public servants and elected officials for review. Approve it, and the rule updates everywhere at once. Red tape becomes a maintenance task, performed continuously, under human direction.

blocks.label: The briefing, recompressed
blocks.body: The simulation compresses the staged transformation of the government estate into eight narrated minutes, in either language, with the architecture moving on screen instead of described in prose.

blocks.text: The code which ships with these white papers is intended to form a foundation for future exploration. We will continue to develop these tools with feedback from stakeholders and share the latest presentation and simulation methods. Our intent is to make each presentation data-driven and reusable. Everything you watched is declared in a single data file: the actors as simple geometric shapes, the chapters, the narration in both languages, the captions, the camera positions, and every timed movement. The engine reads the file, renders the world, and builds one animation timeline per chapter. The narration audio is the master clock. Each step is placed at a fraction of its chapter, and the timeline is scaled to the real length of the recording, so the voice and the motion stay locked together at any point, in either language, even when you scrub.

This design follows the same rule as the rest of the collection: content lives in data, behaviour lives in one engine. Creating a new simulation only requires a new JSON file. The shapes are deliberately simple, rectangles, circles, and small figures, because the information is in the structure and the motion, and the restraint keeps the picture legible to a first-time viewer. For readers who cannot or prefer not to watch animation, the same file drives a chapter-by-chapter storyboard with identical narration, captions, and a full transcript.
